activities.markdown:
# Double Bass Drumming: Two Feet, One Groove

Double bass drumming uses either two bass drums or a double pedal on one bass drum. The slave pedal (left foot) mirrors the master pedal (right foot), giving you independent control of both bass drum beaters.

**Two techniques:**

1. **Heel-toe** (single motion, two sounds): Rock the foot from heel to toe in one motion to produce two consecutive strikes. Efficient at medium tempos and for rolling passages.

2. **Swivel/slide**: Similar single-leg technique for producing rapid double notes.

3. **Alternating feet**: Simply alternating RLRL on the pedals — the most natural starting point. Less efficient at extreme speeds but more controllable for beginners.

**Setup:** Both pedals must be adjusted identically — same beater angle, same spring tension, same beater distance. Any asymmetry in the pedal setup will translate directly into rhythmic unevenness.

**The most important practice tool:** a metronome. Double bass work that's slightly uneven sounds terrible. Tempo precision is not negotiable.

activities.title: Basic Double Bass 8th Notes
activities.description: Alternating RLRL double bass on every 8th note. Snare stays on beats 2 and 4. Hi-hat plays straight 8ths on the right hand. This is the foundational double bass pattern — build it slowly and evenly before adding speed.

activities.title: Double Bass 16th Notes
activities.description: 16th-note double bass: RLRL alternating at twice the density. The hands play 8th notes on hi-hat (typical metal approach). Snare on beats 2 and 4. This is the core of most metal drumming at moderate tempos.

activities.question: When setting up a double pedal, why must both pedals be adjusted identically?
choices: So both feet experience the same resistance, ensuring rhythmic evenness
To make the pedals last longer
activities.explanation: If the two pedals have different spring tension, beater angle, or travel distance, the two feet will have different mechanical resistance. This creates rhythmic unevenness that's clearly audible — the two feet must feel identical to play evenly.
